coredata.dc:title: Two-dimensional MXene incorporating for electron and hole transport in high-performance perovskite solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Aftab, Sikandar
Abbas, Aumber
Iqbal, Muhammad Zahir
Hussain, Sajjad
Kabir, Fahmid
Hegazy, Hosameldin Helmy
Xu, Fan
Kim, Jae Hong
Goud, Burragoni Sravanthi
coredata.dc:description: Over the last decade, perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have made unheard-of strides in increasing power conversion efficiency, making them one of the most promising photovoltaic technologies. However, many obstacles remain to overcome before PSCs can be commercialized, including lead toxicity and the stability problem. It has been discovered that the 2D material MXene, composed of transition metal carbides or nitrides, has a high surface area and excellent electrical conductivity. This material has shown promise for electron and hole transport in PSCs. The main points of the review paper stress the importance of MXene in acting as an electron transport layer and a hole transport layer. It is strongly suggested that MXene can be used in PSCs to achieve the best possible performance levels. It has been demonstrated that MXene can significantly improve the effectiveness and stability of these devices by taking advantage of its unique qualities. MXene is, therefore, probably going to continue to be a strong contender in creating high-performance solar cells in the future. In general, the review paper emphasizes the expanding potential and interest of MXene in energy materials, particularly in solar cells and other renewable energy technologies. MXenes may become more significant as this field of study develops, contributing to creating effective and long-lasting energy solutions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Over the last decade, perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have made unheard-of strides in increasing power conversion efficiency, making them one of the most promising photovoltaic technologies. However, many obstacles remain to overcome before PSCs can be commercialized, including lead toxicity and the stability problem. It has been discovered that the 2D material MXene, composed of transition metal carbides or nitrides, has a high surface area and excellent electrical conductivity. This material has shown promise for electron and hole transport in PSCs. The main points of the review paper stress the importance of MXene in acting as an electron transport layer and a hole transport layer. It is strongly suggested that MXene can be used in PSCs to achieve the best possible performance levels. It has been demonstrated that MXene can significantly improve the effectiveness and stability of these devices by taking advantage of its unique qualities. MXene is, therefore, probably going to continue to be a strong contender in creating high-performance solar cells in the future. In general, the review paper emphasizes the expanding potential and interest of MXene in energy materials, particularly in solar cells and other renewable energy technologies. MXenes may become more significant as this field of study develops, contributing to creating effective and long-lasting energy solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The photovoltaic (PV) effect is a mechanism that allows PV solar cells to produce direct current electricity from solar energy [1,2]. Such devices have garnered much interest from the academic community and are an up-and-coming source of energy generation. PV solar cells such as first-generation crystalline silicon solar cells, second-generation thin film solar cell technology, and third generation or emerging solar cell technology, i.e. dye-sensitized solar cells, organic solar cells, III–V solar cells quantum dot (QD)-sensitized solar cells, and perovskite solar cells (PSCs) are examples of some of the renewable energy sources that have been contributed to the environment. Crystalline silicon can be easily obtained, is non-toxic, and allows for the development of stable solar cells with a conversion efficiency of up to 27%. For these reasons, silicon solar cells have dominated the industry for so long. High temperature fabrication process, energy-intensive manufacturing processes, and a substantial worldwide demand for Si act as obstacles hindering the development of this cutting-edge silicon solar cell technology. Crystalline solar cells must have a structure that is continuous and has few grain boundaries that permit the simple movement of photo-generated carriers in order to operate efficiently. Creating highly efficient PV solar cells is primarily motivated by commercial interests to lower fabrication costs and produce high-quality semiconductors. PSCs have quickly become one of these candidates. Thin-film technologies like polycrystalline silicon face challenges from the development of PSC technology. High power conversion efficiencies (PCEs) can be attained by using straightforward, inexpensive processing [3]. In order to improve PCE and stability of PSCs, two-dimensional (2D) graphene and transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) have in fact been the subject of extensive research [1,4,5]. Incorporating graphene or TMDs into PSCs seeks to solve two significant problems: enhancing PCE and enhancing device stability [6,7]. Researchers' interest in PSCs has increased over the last decade due to their high efficiency, low fabrication cost, and straightforward manufacturing process [8–10]. According to certification, PSCs' PCE can reach up to 25.5% which have proven to perform better than commercial multi-crystalline silicon solar cells [11–13]. The significant advancements in this area show the promise of PSCs, even though their PCEs are still lower than those of single-crystalline silicon solar cells [14–16]. Furthermore, PSCs are theoretically more efficient than 30%, suggesting that PCE may yet prove revolutionary [17,18]. The typical elements of a PSC are a transparent conducting oxide, a perovskite active layer, a metal electrode, an electron transport layer (ETL), and a hole transport layer (HTL). The ETL is crucial for affecting the perovskite layer's quality in addition to extracting electrons and blocking holes. However, the ETL and HTL, which are crucial for PSC performance, must be satisfied with high electron transfer efficiency and hole transfer efficacy, respectively [15,17,19,20]. MXene has been studied in the context of PSCs for two key roles: as an additive in the perovskite active layer and as an interlayer between the electrode and the HTL. MXene can be added to the perovskite active layer as an additive to improve its performance. The addition of MXene nanosheets to the perovskite film improves charge extraction, decreases recombination losses, and improves PSC stability. MXene promotes efficient charge transfer and collection, resulting in improved PCEs and device stability. Furthermore, in PSCs, MXene can act as an interlayer between the electrode and the HTL. A more favorable energy level alignment at the electrode–HTL interface can be achieved by introducing an MXene interlayer, allowing for more efficient charge extraction and lowering interfacial resistance. MXene interlayers can also reduce ion and moisture diffusion, improving device stability. These interlayers act as electron and ion transporters, enhancing the charge extraction and overall device performance. The effect of MXene incorporation on device performance, stability, and charge transport properties is investigated.

2D materials, known as MXenes, have drawn significant interest recently due to their potential use in different industries, including energy storage, sensing, and catalysis. They come from the transition metal carbide, nitride, and carbonitride family, which has layered structures that can be selectively etched to produce nanosheets with distinct physical and chemical properties [21,22]. Mn+1XnTx (n = 1, 2, 3) is the general formula for MXenes, which are 2D structures with one or more layers. M is an abbreviation for an early transition metal, X is an atom of carbon or nitrogen, and T stands for surface functionalization groups (-F, –O, –OH) in single or multilayer 2D structures [23]. The electronic structure of MXenes and the electrostatic potential close to surfaces are profoundly affected by the large shifts in work function (WF) of these groups (from 1.6 to 6.25 eV for O termination) [24]. More significantly, when MXenes are added as dopants to precursor solutions for perovskite absorbers or charge-transporting layers (CTLs), these materials can alter their WF. This was utilized in the production of efficient n-i-p PSCs with PCEs greater than 20% [24]. The energy level alignment and, ultimately, the PCE of the cell are greatly enhanced by the tuning of materials WF, which is crucial in producing the proper interfacial dipoles at the perovskite/CTL interfaces. MXenes are widely used in many different fields, as a result of their exceptional conductivity, flexibility, hydrophilicity, and tunable surface terminations, as well as their ability to hybridize with other types of materials [25–27]. MXenes are widely used in microwave absorption [28,29], energy storage [28], sensors [30], and catalysis [31,32]. In addition, Ti3C2Tx, a typical and extensively researched MXene, has been incorporated into PSCs for use as electrodes, additives, interlayers, HTLs, and ETLs, among other things [33–35]. MXene materials have a number of obvious advantages, such as WF and tunable high conductivity, which make it easier for these materials to be utilized in optoelectronic devices [36]. Additionally, MXenes have been researched for use in charge extraction layers for PSCs.

To address the electron transfer efficiency, many efforts have already been made to enhance PSC performance, including the use of QDs [16,37–39], interfacial engineering [37–41], dimensional control [40,41], and compositional optimization [41–43]. It has been demonstrated that introducing the additive is a workable strategy to improve performance of the device. It is worth noting that applying the additive in the ETLs was largely responsible for the reported improvements in PSCs' performance [8]. In order to improve the efficiency of electron transfer, a wide variety of substances, such as polymers, fullerenes, quaternary ammonium ions, and their derivatives, as well as QDs, have been investigated and found to be effective [44–46]. For instance, Wang et al.'s [47] use of fullerene derivatives as ETLs produced outstanding stability and a champion PCE of 18.39%. Additionally, Li et al. [45] reported the development of a high-quality ETL made of carbon QDs and TiO2 nano colloid for the first time in 2017. A more effective carrier extraction is suggested by the modified ETL's shorter time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) lifetime. The utilization of carbon dots to improve the device stability was discussed in a recent article authored by Hui et al. [48], where they created a composite ETL using SnO2 and carbon dots. The electron mobility dramatically increased by nearly twenty times, going from 9.32 × 10−4 to 1.73 × 10−2 cm2/V/s, according to the novel ETL. The resulting PCE was 22.77%, and after 1000 h, the humidity stability could be maintained at 95% under a humidity range of 40–60% at 25 °C. These findings suggest that using additives to create efficient ETLs is a simple yet effective approach to improving carrier separation and electron extraction in many types of high-performance PSCs and other common perovskite-based optoelectronic devices. To simultaneously enhance PSC functionality and stability, Chen et al., [49] introduce 2D titanium-carbide MXenes that are incredibly thin (Ti3C2T x QD) and Cu1.8S nanocrystals. They surpass reference devices' 18.31% hysteresis-free PCE by a remarkable margin of 21.64%, with noticeably higher long-term stability. The potential of Ti3C2T x MXene as an ETL for effective conventional architecture PSCs has been demonstrated using a room-temperature solution process [50].

In particular, the HTL is of critical importance to the performance and longevity of PSCs. The electrical properties of PEDOT:PSS-based HTL have been improved using a variety of techniques, such as doping [51,52], metal oxide nanocrystals like NiOx [53,54], bilayer HTL [55,56], CuSCN [57], and CuOx [57], as well as polymers like PEDOT:PSS [51,58,59] and poly(bis(4-phenyl) (2,4,6-trimethylphenyl)amine) (PTAA) [60]. The JSC of PSCs is improved by doping them with highly conductive materials like carbon nano-onions [61] and silver nanowires [62]. According to PTAA, the highest certified PCE for single-junction planar inverted PSCs is 24.3% [63]. However, the application of PTAA needs to be improved by its high cost and complicated process, particularly for large-area PSCs [64]. High transparency, adaptability, and low-temperature solution-process compatibility make PEDOT:PSS the most widespread commercial polymer-based hole transporting material (HTM) in PSCs [65]. For the time being, hydrophilic PEDOT:PSS is advantageous for making films using large-area printing. However, PEDOT:PSS has poor stability, insufficient conductivity, and an energy level incompatible with the perovskite active layer [66,67]. These will result in low short-circuit current density (JSC) and open-circuit voltage (VOC) [68]. High conductivity and tunable WF are just two of the obvious advantages MXene materials have that make it easier to use them in optoelectronic devices [36]. As an addition to a perovskite absorber layer [69], Ti3C2Tx MXene is first used in PSCs. It has been suggested that adding MXene to nanoparticles like SnO2 [70] and TiO2 [49] is a practical way to create PSCs with an efficient ETL [34]. [49,71] As an interlayer in PSCs, Ti3C2 MXenes have also been used outside of ETL and HTL [72,73]. MXene served as a defense against moisture and other elements that can damage perovskite films. Additionally, the MXene-coated perovskite film displayed improved PL and charge transport characteristics, demonstrating that the MXene improved the PSC's overall performance. For instance, the performance of the device was significantly enhanced when MXene Ti3C2Tx was used as an HTL in a PSC. The PSCs overall efficiency was raised and its current density increased as a result of the MXene HTL. The performance of the device was enhanced by using MXene Ti3C2Tx as an ETL in a PSC, which also produced a high VOC. The comparison of various parameters used as ETLs and HTLs for PSCs is shown in Tables 1 and 2
, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2D MXene ETLs for PSCs
MXenes for inverted PSCs based on NiO
ce:section.ce:para: Saranin et al. [89] provide evidence for MXene doping's beneficial effects on the ETL and perovskite absorber in NiO-based inverted PSCs (see Fig. 1). Passivating trap states within the cell structure is made possible by the presence of MXenes, which improves charge extraction and collection at the electrodes and makes it simple to change the energy level alignment at perovskite/CTL interfaces. The MXene-based engineered cells demonstrated superior performance in comparison to reference devices, with PCE exceeding 19%. The proposed method opens up a lot of ways to make inverted PSC structures, which are very promising in terms of their future large-area device compatibility and stability over the long-term. This is due to the WF of MXenes' ability to be tailored during their chemical synthesis and the fact that, when used as a dopant, they can alter the optoelectronic characteristics of PSC layers. The design and investigation of four distinct device structures that vary from one another in terms of the MXene doping of constituent layers. Figs. 2 a and b show a schematic of the structure of the examined device and the corresponding energy band diagram. The average VOC values did, in fact, slightly decline in all the structures using MXenes, but this was not enough to counteract the significant JSC increase, which improved PCE across the board. Fig. 2c illustrates the J–V curves for the optimal cell in each investigated structure. Quasi-Fermi level splitting (QFLS) in the absorbing layer is commonly linked to VOC in solar cells and PSCs [92]. Despite the fact that the VOC levels that are typically measured are significantly lower, the QFLS value should theoretically correspond to the VOC value. Perovskite and the transport layer have an energetic offset along with a higher interfacial recombination rate provide a consistent explanation for the significant discrepancy between the QFLS and the VOC. The quasi-Fermi levels consequently propagate flatly over most of the absorber, but there is potential for the perovskite/CTL interface exhibits substantial bending [93]. This image makes it abundantly clear that interface engineering can significantly influence how the VOC is managed in PSCs. There was a small average VOC drop in the devices after MXenes were added to the perovskite (or alternatively, PCBM) layer. Theoretical simulation results point to a stronger band bending at the NiO/perovskite (or alternatively, perovskite/PCBM) interface as the cause of this drop [89]. In contrast, a VOC averaged value similar to that obtained for reference devices (PSC-A) was achieved by including the WF shift and the combined effects of enhancing the charge transport characteristics in PCBM and minimizing trap-mediated recombination in MAPbI3 (PSC-D). Fig. 2d shows a significant rise in the incident photon to current conversion efficiency after MXenes were introduced to the PCBM and perovskite layers (PSC-D), demonstrating the MXenes significant effect on the JSC of the device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MXenes modified SnO2 to create stable and efficient PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Niu et al. [85] introduce Nb2C MXenes as an external SnO2 ETL additive, resulting in a noticeable growth of SnO2 grains. The PSC fabrication schematic is depicted in Fig. 3 a to demonstrate how adding SnO2–Nb2C can enhance PSC performance. SnO2–Nb2C-based ETL has better surface energy, roughness, and defects than a control SnO2 film because Nb2C MXenes can increase the lattice spacing of the facets of SnO2. Perovskite films show improved quality with more crystalline and efficient carrier transport after being deposited over the SnO2–Nb2C ETL. PSCs based on SnO2–Nb2C ETL have a champion PCE of 22.86%, outperforming the control devices by a wide margin of 18.96%; after 40 days at 25 °C with 40–60% humidity, these target devices retain 98% of their initial efficiencies (Fig. 3b). The shelf-life stability of the PSCs was studied at 25 °C, 40–60% relative humidity, and complete darkness to understand better how Nb2C MXenes affect the stability of the device. The results for the two devices without encapsulation are shown in Fig. 3c. A total of 40 days after initial exposure, the stability of the SnO2–Nb2C ETL-based PSC (represented by the red curve) had decreased by only about 2%, while the stability of the SnO2 ETL-based control PSC had decreased by about 15%, demonstrating the Nb2C MXenes' significant contribution to the PSCs' stability. It provides insight into the design and preparation of a modified electron transfer layer to enhance the efficiency and durability of PSCs and similar perovskite-based photoelectric conversion electronic devices.

Zheng et al. [82] created a hybrid film that combines SnO2 nanoparticles and Ti3C2Tx MXene nanoflakes for use as the PSC's ETL. Unencapsulated devices have much greater environmental stability, and the PCE of PSCs has increased significantly from 16.28 to 20.35% (Fig. 3d). Fig. 3e displays the steady output of PSCs for 180 s at their maximum power point (MPP) under 100 W m−2 of monochromatic light with 20-s light on/off cycles. The MXene-incorporated cell shows better cell stability than the control unit. The viability of commercializing PSCs is heavily reliant on their ability to maintain environmental stability. Using the devices that were kept in a controlled environment at 25 °C and 30% relative-humidity (RH), the stability of the unencapsulated devices is assessed (Fig. 3f). In contrast, the control device lost 41% of its initial efficiency after 456 h. This finding implies that the MXene addition may improve the device's stability. The device's PCE with MXene integration maintained roughly 74% of its initial value after 768 h. It is reasonable to assume that the performance and stability of PSCs are significantly influenced by the defect density of perovskite films. The perovskite layer's film quality as well as the characteristics of the perovskite/ETL interface are significantly improved by using an MXene/SnO2 hybrid film as the ETL in PSCs, which simultaneously reduces bulk defects and interface defects. As a result, the hybrid ETL significantly improves the device PCE and stability when compared to the pristine SnO2 ETL. An easy, dependable, and a successful method of reducing the flaws in perovskite films is to use the recently developed MXene nanomaterials. Other optoelectronic devices based on perovskites might also benefit from these materials.

Wang et al. [83] suggested that incorporating 2D MXene with strong interface interaction can improve the SnO2 ETL's ability to conduct efficient charge transfers. The PSCs in their configured state are displayed in Fig. 3g. Due to its reduced trap densities, the MXene-modified SnO2 ETL provides a more favorable growth substrate for perovskite films. In MXene-modified SnO2-based PSCs, using a spatially resolved imaging technique, it is possible to observe significantly reduced charge transport and non-radiative recombination losses as well. Therefore, the PSC achieves a negligibly high saturated current density and little hysteresis, allowing it to operate at an improved efficiency of 20.65%. Fig. 3h shows the champion device (0.5 mg/mL MXene) has PCE of 20.65% with greatly enhanced JSC of 24.34 mA/cm2 and a VOC of 1.11 V. This was achieved primarily as a result of improved band alignment and improved electron transport of MXene-modified SnO2 ETL. The MXene-modified device in Fig. 3i has a stabilized operating efficiency of 20.47% and an output JSC of 21.81 mA/cm2, which is near the MPP. A comprehensive understanding of the mechanisms underlying MXene interface engineering, which offers an alternative method for the production of efficient PSCs.

Yang et al. [86] use SnO2 to create a nanoscale heterojunction, and in-situ oxidized Ti3C2T x (O–Ti3C2T x ) MXene was added as an ETL in a MAPbI3 PSC. The FTO/ETL/CH3NH3PbI3/Sprio-OMeTAD/Ag-structured devices shown in Fig. 3j were created to test the PV performance of devices based on the O–Ti3C2T x /SnO2 heterojunction. Fig. 3k displays the reverse and forward scans of the J–V curves for PSCs based on SnO2 and O–Ti3C2T x /SnO2. The PCE rose from 17.68% to 20.09% thanks to the O–Ti3C2T x /SnO2 heterojunction allowed for increased electron extraction while simultaneously reducing the amount of recombination that occurred between the ETL and the perovskite. This also improved stability in air. Fig. 3l shows the photocurrents measured for devices based on O–Ti3C2T x /SnO2 at MPP (0.78 and 0.86 V) for more than 500 s. The photocurrent curves, power outputs, and error bars were all rather close to the J–V curves.

Yang et al. [33] investigated the possibility of utilizing Ti3C2 MXene in organic–inorganic lead halide PV energy conversion devices. Those devices that have ITO/ETL/CH3NH3PbI3/Spiro-OMeTAD/Ag structure in order to assess the potential of Ti3C2 to enhance PSC efficiency (Fig. 3m). In Fig. 3n, the J–V curves for PSCs with SnO2, SnO2–Ti3C2, or Ti3C2 as ETLs are displayed. With increasing Ti3C2 loading, the value of JSC steadily raises from 22.83 to 23.56 mA/cm2. In some circumstances, this has a favorable impact on VOC and FF. The Ti3C2 concentration of 1.0 wt% produced the best results, with maximum values of VOC and FF and a PCE of more than 18%. In composite ETL-like conductive wires, it is thought that metallic Ti3C2 flakes act to improve the electron transport pathways, resulting in more efficient electron collection. The JSC continues to rise as a result. Although the JSC value increased with increasing Ti3C2 concentration (greater than 1.0 wt), the PCE decreased; this was likely due to a decrease in VOC and FF levels. Additionally, the device that used pure Ti3C2 as the ETL only displayed 5.28% PCE. Perovskites' time-resolved photoluminescence spectra based on various ETLs are shown in Fig. 3q. The PL decay lifetimes of perovskites based on SnO2, SnO2–Ti3C2 (1.0 wt‰), and Ti3C2 are, in that order, 40.3, 33.8, and 42.7 ns, respectively. This demonstrates that the incorporation of Ti3C2 to SnO2 results in a sizeable increase in the JSC, which lends further support to the findings obtained from the J–V curves and external quantum efficiency spectra, both of which agree with the steady-state PL results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MXene QDs improve moisture stability and PSC crystallinity
ce:section.ce:para: Liu et al. [95] using a one-step deposition technique, the Cl-terminated Ti3C2 quantum dots (also known as Ti3C2Clx QDs) are added to the perovskite precursor solution as additives. The configuration of the device is shown in Fig. 4 a. Fig. 4b shows the J–V curves and PV characteristics for both the champion device and the pristine device (with 0.2 mg/mL Ti3C2Clx QDs). As can be seen, the addition of Ti3C2Clx QDs reduced the hysteresis in J-V between in both reverse and forward scan. After the addition of 0.2 mg/mL Ti3C2Clx QDs, the hysteresis index dropped from 0.628 to 0.197. The charge accumulation between the interfaces, which can be driven by ion migration, excessive defect concentrations, or unbalanced charge transport, has been suggested to be the primary cause of PSC hysteresis. After the addition of 0.2 mg/mL Ti3C2Clx QDs, the charge accumulation is shown to be reduced by a decrease in the hysteresis index. This is due, in part, to the perovskite film's higher crystallinity, which results in fewer grain boundaries, which inhibit ion migration and lower defect concentrations. Fig. 4c shows the PCE changes over time (for 1000 h) for the corresponding devices without encapsulation, confirming the reports' perspectives and the devices' long-term operating stability. For this monitoring, 40% relative humidity, complete darkness, and room temperature were required. According to previous studies, after 1000 h of ageing, the unencapsulated device containing Ti3C2OHx QDs or Ti3C2Tx QDs showed inferior long-term stability compared to the pristine device. Additionally, the Ti3C2OHx QD-equipped device showed the worst stability (PCE dropped by over 65%), supporting the detrimental effects of the hydroxyl terminations. The unencapsulated Ti3C2Clx QD device, in contrast, demonstrated exceptional long-term stability against humidity; after 1000 h of aging, it still retained more than 84% of its initial PCE. The Cl-terminated Ti3C2Clx QDs' advantage in PSCs was clearly shown by the stability test.

Chen et al. [75] use ultrathin 2D MXenes Ti3C2T x QDs by designing the perovskite/TiO2 ETL interface, perovskite absorber, and adding Cu1.8S nanocrystals to optimize the Spiro-OMeTAD HTL is represented. Fig. 4d shows a schematic representation of the entire heterojunction PSC device structure along with a structure. The J–V curves for the TiO2/perovskite (Control), TiO2/TQD/perovskite (S1), and TiO2/TQD-perovskite (S2) cells are shown in Fig. 4e. It is evident that the PCE for the device with TQD added to the m-TiO2 reaches 19.65%, as opposed to 18.31% for the device without TQD. The TiO2/TQD/TQD-perovskite cell with VOC = 1.13 V, JSC = 23.64 mA/cm2, FF = 77.56%, and a PCE of 20.72% is the optimized device in all other respects. A full simulation of solar illumination (AM1.5, 100 mW/cm2) was used to perform the stabilized power output and current of PSCs at their MPP (0.85, 0.89, and 0.92 V, respectively) (Fig. 4f). After 200 s of illumination, the JSC and PCE of the unencapsulated devices have stabilized. In accordance with the decreased hysteresis, the PSCs based on TiO2/TQD/perovskite cells have a greatly quicker response time of 2 s to stabilize power output than the unaltered device (20 s). It has to do with how change mobility increased after TQD was added, while trap-assisted recombination decreased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Planar p–i–n PSCs: MXene-based carrier dynamics, defect passivation, and band alignment
ce:section.ce:para: Chava et al. [84] use Ti3C2T x to customize the electronic properties of the ETL and ETL/perovskite interface in inverted (p–i–n) PSCs and then correlate those electronic properties to the measured PCE. Improvements in electrical conductivity and band alignment at the ETL/perovskite interface are brought about by MXene doping in [6,6]-phenyl- C61-butyric acid methyl ester (M-PC61BM)-based ETLs. Fig. 5 a depicts a cross-sectional schematic of the control device's architecture. The HTL in each device was made of NiOx. Figs. 5b and c show results of the J–V and quantum efficiency characterization for the test (A-C) and control (D) devices. Each PSC device's integrated JSC from EQE spectra agrees with the findings of the J–V measurement. Based on these J–V and EQE values, device A (M-PC61BM as ETL) had the most efficient configuration of the four evaluated devices, with a PCE of 18%. Device A's PV specs were JSC = 22.8 mA/cm2, VOC = 1.04 V, and FF = 0.76 (with M-PC61BM as ETL). Devices B, C, and D all performed poorly in comparison to device A, with PCEs of 15.7%, 1.6%, and 15%, respectively. The most important factor affecting device performance in each MXene introduction mode should also be identified. In light of these results, Ti3C2T x MXene has been proposed as a promising material for the development of cutting-edge electrical and optoelectronic devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plasma-oxidized Ti3C2Tx MXene for efficient PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Wang et al. [87] used a solution-based process performed at room temperature, followed by an oxygen plasma treatment to use Ti3C2T x MXene as the ETL in PSCs. Ti3C2T x MXene's crystalline structure is schematically depicted in Fig. 6 a both prior to and followed by the treatment with oxygen plasma. The device J–V curves as well as the PCE statistics are displayed in Figs. 6b and c, respectively. Following a 3-min plasma oxidation of MXene, the device had the highest level of performance possible. The increased VOC and FF, which were probably connected to the tunable WF or/and decreased states in an interface trap, are primarily responsible for the PCE improvement. Fig. 6d displays the best-performing device's steady-state power output at a voltage bias of 0.88 V near the MPP. The output PCE of 18.9% and steady JSC of 21.5 mA/cm2 are produced by one of the highest reported performance for MXene-based PSCs to date. Fig. 6e shows how the device stability has also been routinely inspected. Without being enclosed, the devices were at room temperature in ambient air conditions (25 °C, relative humidity 50%). Within 700 h, the reference device with pure MXene had lost 10% of its initial efficiency. The plasma-oxidized MXene-based device, on the other hand, demonstrated better stability, after 750 h of storage, retaining approximately 80% of the initial PCE. Additionally, compared to devices without plasma treatment, the MXene-based devices that had been plasma oxidized showed improved photostability. These findings suggest that appropriate plasma oxidation-induced the formation of Ti–O on MXene could make the device more stable.

This was probably caused by the abundance of oxygen functional groups, like –OH and –O that were present on the surface of MXene and may have permitted interactions between the poorly coordinated Pb atoms in the perovskite and MXene. The research would contribute to our understanding of MXene's surface modification and its potential use in optoelectronics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Air-oxidized Ti3C2Tx improves MXene-based PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Yang et al. [80] used oxidation method to modify the properties of Ti3C2T x hydrocolloid for ETL in low-temperature processed PSCs. To evaluate Ti3C2T x 's performance before and after oxidation, as well as after combining the two as the ETL in PSCs. A typical SEM image of a cross-section through a PSC device that was manufactured with a Ti3C2T x layer functioning as an ETL is shown in Fig. 7 a. This image allowed researchers to determine the ETL layer's thickness to be 20 ± 3 nm. Fig. 7b shows J–V curves of PSCs based on Ti3C2T x , LO-Ti3C2T x , HO–Ti3C2T x , HO–Ti3C2T x @Ti3C2T x and TiO2 under standard testing conditions. The improvement in JSC and FF was due to the PCE of the device based on LO-Ti3C2T x (17.09%) being a little higher than that of Ti3C2T x (16.50%). Higher JSC and FF are achieved by LO–Ti3C2T x because the TiO2 makes the film surface more homogeneous while maintaining due to its monolayer design and high mobility of Ti3C2T x . As previously mentioned, when the Ti3C2T x underwent severe oxidation, the 2D structure was largely substituted by TiO2 nanoparticles. As a result, the material's conductivity was reduced, which caused the JSC and FF to decrease. The device based on HO– Ti3C2T x had a PCE of 15.52% as a result. Fig. 7c depicts EQE spectrums and related integrated current densities.

To investigate the kinetics of SnO2 ETL-modulated perovskite crystallization, ,Yang et al. [78] describe a synchrotron-based in situ 2D grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction technique. The perovskite nucleation from the precursor solution was found to be quickly induced by the Ti3C2T x -MXene QDsSnO2 (MQDs-SnO2) ETL, which then formed an intermediate perovskite phase after anti-solvent treatment. As a result, the perovskite film's crystal quality and phase stability are significantly improved. Configuration of fabricated PSC devices, which can be seen in Fig. 7d. For the reference device, Fig. 7e displays a PCE of 20.96% along with a JSC of 24.26 mA/cm2, a VOC of 1.140 V, and a FF of 0.758. A maximum PCE of 23.34% was attained by the champion target device, which had a JSC=24.96 mA/cm2, VOC = 1.172 V, and FF = 0.798. It should be noted that no obvious hysteresis between forward and reverse scan was detected for the target device, which may be related to the MQDs-SnO2's superior electron extraction and transport capabilities [78]. The stability of the unencapsulated PSC devices over time when they are kept in a certain environment with a relative humidity of 40%–60% at 25 °C is shown in Fig. 7f. When compared to the reference PSC, the PCE of the target cell held steady at around 90% after 500 h of storage, while the reference PSC dropped to around 80%. Perovskite films produced on the MQDs-SnO2ETL showed improved stability in the face of light, moisture, heat, and oxygen, as determined by the stability tests.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single-layered TiO2-doped MXene nanosheets for efficient and stable double PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Li et al. [79] use a straightforward method to incorporate multifunctional ETL single-layered MXene nanosheets into titania for reliable and effective Cs2AgBiBr6 double PSCs (Ti3C2T x @TiO2). TiO2's electron extraction rate and electrical conductivity are greatly improved by the addition of single-layered MXene nanosheets, which also change the wettability of the ETL's surface and promote the crystallization of Cs2AgBiBr6 double PSC devices. Figs. 8 a and b show the device configuration in order to study the impact of Ti3C2Tx@TiO2 as ETL on the PSC of Cs2AgBiBr6. Fig. 8c displays the PV performance of PSCs using a J–V measurement under one sun illumination conditions. The Ti3C2Tx@TiO2 noticeably enhances the performance of device when compared to TiO2. Due to the Ti3C2Tx@TiO2 ETL's increased electron mobility and better energy alignment, the JSC increased from 3.29 mA/cm2 to 4.14 mA/cm2, the FF improved from 0.65 to 0.7, and the PCE significantly augmented from 2.0% to 2.81%. Single-layered MXene nanosheets' high conductivity makes it easier to extract and transfer electrons while reducing non-radiative recombination sites (Fig. 8d). The findings demonstrate that the Ti3C2Tx@TiO2 ETL not only enhances PSCs' JSC but also completely removes hysteresis in the photocurrent. A comparison of the device stability of PSCs based on TiO2 and Ti3C2Tx@TiO2 is shown in Fig. 8e. In a controlled laboratory environment (humidity of 10%), PSCs' long-term stability was investigated. After 15 days of being exposed to an environment without a protective covering, the PCE of the TiO2-based device lost 22% of its initial PCE. However, Ti3C2Tx@TiO2-based device maintains 93% of its original PCE. This result shows that the double PSCs' photostability is significantly improved by the single-layered MXene nanosheets.

Huang et al. [77] developed and applied a multi-dimensional conductive heterojunction structure constructed of TiO2, SnO2, and Ti3C2T X to produce efficient and dependable planar PSCs. Fig. 8f depicts the entire device structure of the PSC. Fig. 8g depicts the typical J–V curves. The MDCN-Air&N2 ETL-based device is still seen to exhibit the highest PCE when compared to other devices. The devices are tested in air with a 30–40% relative humidity at ambient conditions to determine the PSC's ability to withstand moisture. Fig. 8h shows that after 45 days, over 85% of the device's initial PCE has been maintained, indicating the great stability of the MDCN-Air&N2 ETL. Contrarily, in the same test environment, the device with SnO2 ETL performs at less than 75% of its initial capacity. Because of its one-of-a-kind MDCN structure beneath the ETL, the perovskite layer exhibits exceptional moisture resistance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MXene-nanotube hybrids for efficient PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Bati, et al. [98] prepared MXene/single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNT) composites using sorted metallic single-walled carbon nanotubes (m-SWCNTs) to improve PSCs' PV performance. Fig. 9 a shows the schematic illustration of the device structure employed for the study. The built-in devices adhere to the ITO/SnO2/(FAPbI3) x (MAPbBr3)1−x /Spiro-OMeTAD/Au planar structure. The inset shows a spin-coated MXene/m-SWCNT composite on top of a SnO2 layer. The J–V curves of the highest-performing devices, both with and without Mxene/m-SWCNT interlayers, are shown in Fig. 9b. To investigate the performance of the PV, PSCs were fabricated using interfacial layers that contained varying proportions of MXene and m-SWCNTs. These PSCs were then subjected to measurement under the standard conditions. The important PV parameter PCE can be found in Fig. 9c. Compared to control cells that were constructed without the application of any interfacial layers, the PCEs of the devices significantly increased after interfacial layers being introduced. These improvements were primarily made possible by the FF parameter in particular. The existence of nanotubes and efficient passivation of oxygen vacancies at the surface of the SnO2 layer likely accounts for these enhancements, which may be attributed to enhanced charge extraction and injection processes. Xu et al. hypothesized that because of the high oxygen content on the surfaces of nanotubes [99], defects associated with oxygen vacancies can be minimized, thereby decreasing the loss due to non-radiative recombination. Remarkably, the devices that combined MXene and m-SWCNTs at the ideal ratio of 2:1 showed significant improvement, producing a champion device with a VOC of 1.07 V, a JSC of 25.09 mA/cm2, a FF of 0.80, and a PCE of 21.42%. It is important to note that our devices had 0.2 cm2 active areas, which is a sizeable active area. This result indicates that the charge extraction efficiency can be greatly enhanced by adding a small amount of m-SWCNTs to MXene, leading to outstanding device performance. However, the following two scenarios may explain the ineffective efficiency improvement at higher m-SWCNTs ratios. The capacity of nanotubes to transfer electrons in addition to holes, or to be ambipolar, makes them ideal recombination centers [100]. The other one is a process known as back electron transfer that happens because m-SWCNTs' electronic structure lacks an optical band gap and can result in a quick charge recombination [101]. It is important to note that because of the higher recombination rate, increasing the nanotube loadings typically results in lower PV performance efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2D MXene HTLs for PSCs
Modify Nb2CTx MXene's HTL WF for PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: The efficiency of hole transfer at the perovskite/HTL interface and the crystallization of a perovskite film are both significantly influenced by HTL in PSCs. In order to create the inverted PSCs, the Nb2CTx MXene, which was prepared and used as the HTL and has excellent photoelectric properties. By enhancing the –O terminated functional groups on the Nb2CTx surface, oxygen plasma treatment modified the WF of Nb2CTx HTL [105]. The EF differences lead to an upward band-bending at the Nb2CTx/MAPbI3 interface [109]. Further analysis of the band-bending and charge transport mechanism at the Nb2CTx/MAPbI3 interface is shown in Figs. 10 a and b. The MAPbI3's conduction band (Ec = −3.92), valence band (Ev = −5.52), energy gap (Eg = 1.60), and effective energy (EF = −4.48 eV) have all been measured previously [110]. The energy difference between the EF of the Nb2CTx and the Ev or Ec of the perovskite film is used to define the energy barrier for holes (ΔE h) or electrons (ΔE e) transport across the Nb2CTx/MAPbI3 interface. The calculated values for the pure Nb2CTx with a WF of 0.76 eV are (ΔE h = 4.68 eV) and (ΔE e = 0.84 eV). At the Nb2CTx/MAPbI3 interface, there is a significant amount of electron–hole pair recombination because the large ΔE h does not allow for the hole transportation, and the small ΔE e is insufficient to effectively block electrons. Following oxygen plasma treatment, the Nb2CTx has a higher WF of 5.04 eV, and the ΔE h and ΔE e are calculated to be 0.48 and 1.12 eV, respectively. The improved ΔE e and decreased ΔE h make it possible to separate and transport charge carriers more effectively, which helps to lessen charge accumulation and energy loss at the Nb2CTx/MAPbI3 interface [111]. It is important to remember that this change in ΔE h and ΔE e is also advantageous for raising the inherent potential and VOC of PSCs. The light-absorbing layer is chosen to be the MAPbI3, and the ETL is chosen to be the PCBM. Fig. 10c depicts the schematic representation of the device structure as well as the structure of Nb2CTx MXene. The oxygen plasma-treated Nb2CTx HTL is used to fabricate the flexible and large area (0.99 cm2) PSCs with PCE of 17.26% and 17.94% Figs. 10d and e, which demonstrates the Nb2CTx HTL's potential for use in flexible electronics. This shows that the Nb2CTx MXene has excellent potential for use in the field of PVs, particularly in flexible devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spray-deposited Ti3C2Tx MXene HTL for planar PSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Du et al. [103] used the spray technique to deposit Ti3C2T x MXene nanosheets with varying amounts of the silane coupling agent vinyltris (2-methoxyethoxylsilane) (SCA), including 0%, 0.1 V/V%, 0.3 V/V%, 0.5 V/V%, and 0.7 V/V%, were used as the HTL. In recent years, PSC has gradually been subjected to coupling agents as a result of the interface modification properties that they possess. A device with the structural components ITO/HTL/CH3NH3PbI3/PC61BM/Ag was created, as shown in Fig. 11 a, in order to study the impact of various volume fractions of SCA on Ti3C2T x HTL in PSCs. The J–V of PSC is summarized in Fig. 11b. It is evident that the device using Ti3C2T x as the HTL performs poorly. Adding SCA with a volume fraction of 0.3% resulted in improvements in JSC and FF of 21.30 mA/cm2 and 73.47%, respectively. This resulted in a maximum PCE of 13.65%, but the VOC decreased by 11%. The addition of SCA to MXene nanosheets makes them more suitable for transporting holes and reduces the amount of current shunting. The variations in JSC are consistent with the calculated EQE spectrum and integrated current for the devices with or without SCA (Fig. 11c). It is determined that the device's integrated current density without SCA is 17.75 mA/cm2. After incorporating 0.3% SCA into the Ti3C2T x material, the device's integrated current density increased to 19.94 mA/cm2. An efficient method for enhancing the quality of films based on Ti3C2T x , which also opens up additional avenues for the investigation of Ti3C2T x MXene in the near future.

Ti3C2, a more conductive 2D MXene material, has been used to create a back electrode in HTMs and noble-metal-free PSCs. Ti3C2 has an energy level that is comparable to carbon-based materials [107]. The best device's performance (FTO/C&M TiO2/perovskite/Ti3C2) in terms of PVs is shown in Fig. 11d. The highest PCE measured 13.83% and had the following values: 0.95 V for VOC, 22.97 mA/cm2 for JSC, and 0.63 for FF.

When compared to conventional PSCs (FTO/C&M TiO2/Perovskite/Spiro-OMeTAD/Au), as shown in Fig. 11e, devices based on Ti3C2 electrodes perform better over the long-term, especially when kept at room temperature and humidity (30% humidity) for 360 h without any encapsulation. It is quite clear that the PSCs constructed using Ti3C2 electrodes exhibited an exceptionally high level of stability. A thin layer of Ti3C2 (approximately 300 μm) could serve as an encapsulating layer, isolating from the ambient humidity. It suggests a potential use for MXene in the future as well for HTM and the PSCs without noble metals as a potent electrode candidate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-assembled MXene in flexible large-area perovskite modules
ce:section.ce:para: One of the most difficult problems for the commercialization of HTL is to advance HTL to achieve efficient, flexible, and large-area PSCs. Zhang et al. [108] showed that PSCs with high performance over large areas can be made using a self-assembled gradient Ti3C2Tx MXene incorporating PEDOT: PSS HTL by creating glucose-induced MXene redistribution based on half-caramelization. In order to investigate scalable applications, they have developed flexible device based on MG-PEDOT, with the effective area of 15 cm2. High PCE of 17.46% is shown for the PSCs in Fig. 12 a. Further, they increase the scalable MG-PEDOT-based perovskite solar modules (PSMs) effective area to 15 cm2 with a PCE of 16.28% (Fig. 12b), demonstrating strong potential for scalability applications. As shown in Fig. 12c, consider encapsulated PSMs' operational stability based on MG-PEDOT and PEDOT: PSS HTLs at 1.5 a.m. in free air. For 1000 h, the initial PCE of the PSMs based on MG-PEDOT is maintained at 83%. The thermal stability (at 85 °C) of PSCs in free air is shown in Fig. 12d. With MG-PEDOT, the PCE for the devices is much more stable and can be kept for 92% of the time after 50 h. The acidity of PEDOT:PSS, which causes perovskite film degradation and ITO corrosion, is one of the primary causes of the instability of PSCs with inverted structures using pure PEDOT as the HTL [112]. The Ti3C2Tx MXene is made using the minimally intensive layer delamination (MILD) method, and during the synthesis process, it is washed with deionized water to produce a few layers of product with a pH of 7. Increased PSM operational lifetime and reduced corrosion of the PEDOT:PSS photoactive layer are both achieved with the addition of a neutral MXene solution to the nanocomposite [72]. As a result, the flexible PSMs exhibit astonishing levels of both operation and storage (at high temperature) stability after the introduction of MG-PEDOT as the HTL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inverted PSCs with MXene doping and ion beam sputtered NiOx HTL
ce:section.ce:para: Din et al. [106] look into the synergistic interaction between Ti3C2T x MXene doping of CH3NH3PbI3 (MAPI) perovskite layers and high-quality NiOx HTLs deposited by ion beam sputtering on conducting ITO substrates in order to increase the PCE of p-i-n PSCs. Fig. 13 a depicts a schematic of the PSCs under investigation. Using the NiOx layer as the HTL and either MAPI or MAPI that had been doped with MXene as the photoconversion layer, complete PSC devices were created to study the impact of MXene doping on the performance of PSCs. In Fig. 13b, the J–V characteristics of the champion devices are displayed. The best NiOx HTL devices had PCE of 14% and 16% for the undoped and doped devices, respectively, which is an improvement of 14.3%. These devices had identical 18 nm thick NiOx HTLs. After averaging across 12 devices, a 10% PCE improvement was discovered, which is comparable to results from the earlier studies [69,89]. Figs. 13 (c–f) display the statistical data on the PV parameters. These findings directly affect how well inverted PSCs with p–i–n architecture perform when high-quality IBS-deposited HTLs and MXene doping of the perovskite layer are used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summary and prospects
MXene for PSC
ce:section.ce:para: PSCs' low price, simple manufacturing process, and high-PCE make them appealing. In areas with plenty of sunlight, they have the potential to play a significant role in the world's energy mix. PSC performance has increased quickly, with laboratory-scale devices' efficiency rising from 4% in 2009 to over 25% in 2021. Their efficiency of 20% or more puts them on par with conventional silicon-based solar cells. To make PSCs more stable, resilient, and scalable so that they can be produced in large quantities, researchers are also looking into new materials and manufacturing techniques. Traditional solar panels for rooftops and fields, as well as flexible, thin-film solar cells for portable and wearable devices, are examples of applications for PSCs. Although PSC technology has made promising strides, there are still some obstacles to be overcome, such as lowering the lead content of perovskite materials and enhancing long-term stability. Perovskite technology is an exciting area of research and development for renewable energy, though, due to the potential advantages of inexpensive, highly effective solar cells. Researchers have shown that incorporating MXene into the ETL of PSCs can improve performance by facilitating charge transfer and decreasing recombination losses. MXene is also stable in ambient conditions, making it a more long-lasting option for PSCs. More research is needed, however, to fully understand MXene's potential in PSCs and optimize its properties for this application. A broad family of 2D transition metal carbides and nitrides is known as MXenes. MXenes could be used in PVs, especially PSCs. Over the past decade, PSC PCEs have exceeded 25% [1,113]. They need stability improvements to reach their commercial potential. MXenes' chemical and thermal stability could help solve these stability issues. PSCs use MXenes as HTLs and ETLs. MXene-based HTLs and ETLs have been shown to significantly improve PSC stability and efficiency. MXene was investigated as a potential HTL for use in PSCs, and the results showed it to be superior to organic HTL-based PSCs [114]. MXene as an ETL improved PSC stability in high humidity [34,71]. MXenes have a lot of potential for enhancing the reliability and effectiveness of PSCs. The MXene-based electrodes must be improved, and scalable fabrication techniques for MXene-based PSCs must be developed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of MXene
ce:section.ce:para: MXene integration into PV devices holds great promise, and functionalization of MXene materials is a significant area of research [115–117]. An overview of MXene functionalization methods and their use in PVs is mentioned here: the introduction of various functional groups or chemical moieties can functionalize the surface of MXene. The surface characteristics of MXene, such as its hydrophilicity, surface charge, and interaction with other materials, can be changed by functionalization. Covalent bonding, electrochemical grafting, and wet chemistry techniques are some examples of how to do it. Surface functionalization makes it possible for PV devices to work better with other materials, like electrodes or active layers. Doping MXene entails adding foreign atoms or ions to the compound's structure in order to change its electronic characteristics. MXene's electrical conductivity, ability to transfer charges, and capacity for light absorption can all be improved through doping. The performance of PV applications can be improved by using the right dopants to enable effective charge separation, transport, and collection within the device. MXene can be used to create composites or heterostructures with other semiconductor materials. Improved PV performance is made possible by heterostructure formation, which enables the integration of complementary properties from various materials. For instance, hybrid structures with improved light absorption or charge transport properties can be made using MXene in combination with other 2D materials like TMDs. For effective charge extraction and lower recombination losses, the interfaces between MXene and other components in a PV device must be optimized. The surface properties and energy level alignment at the interfaces can be changed by using interface engineering techniques like interfacial layers or interfacial engineering. As a result, charge transfer and extraction are improved, which improves device performance. The materials made of MXene can be used directly as electrodes in PV devices. High electrical conductivity, good mechanical flexibility, and broad optical transparency are just a few benefits of using MXene electrodes. They can be utilized in a variety of solar cell designs, including organic, dye-sensitized, and perovskite solar cells, as transparent conductive electrodes, counter electrodes, or current collectors. MXene can also be incorporated into the active layers of PV devices. MXene-based active layers can contribute to light absorption, charge generation, and transport. The optoelectronic properties can be tuned and the device performance enhanced by adjusting the MXene composition, layer thickness, and processing parameters. The specific functionalization and use of MXene in photovoltaics may depend on the preferred device architecture, target efficiency, and compatibility with other materials, it is important to note. It is anticipated that ongoing research in this area will further examine and improve the functionalization strategies for MXene to enable its efficient use in PV devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential of MXene for ETLs or HTLs
ce:section.ce:para: The early research mainly concentrated on modifying the electronic characteristics of ETLs or perovskite/ETL interfaces through the utilization of the excellent electrical properties of Ti3C2Tx MXene, which the effects of the MXene introduction on other properties of PSCs, such as the quality of the film and perovskite defects, were relatively disregarded. For a PSC to be effective and stable, polycrystalline perovskite films must be defect-free. However, the use of molecular materials in the existing methods of defect reduction suffers from their complicated fabricating steps, instability, and limited effects. Since studies on the use of MXene in PSCs are just getting started, more in-depth research studies are required to promote the PCE and stability of PSCs and to uncover the mechanism. Currently, research on planar heterojunction PSCs is mainly focused on the ETL, which is essential for photocurrent rectification and limiting charge recombination [118]. TiO2, SnO2, ZnO, and WO x are the most common ETL materials used, and among them, SnO2 has emerged as an ETL with a lot of untapped potential. Other ETL materials with superior optical transparency, high electron mobility, and a deep conduction band include ZnO and WO x [119,120]. However, the electrical conductivity of the pure SnO2 ETL is still too low, and there is not enough contact between the different surfaces [121]. It is essential to make the necessary modifications to the SnO2 ETL in order to overcome these drawbacks. A variety of techniques, including doping, bilayer HTL, and other treatments, have been researched to improve the electrical properties of PEDOT: PSS-based HTL [22]. The bilayer strategies reduce traps and non-radiative recombination at the interface. The main difficulty with the two doping and bilayer structures is that they either have undesirable energy-level structures with conductive dopants or impeded conductivity because of the insulating layer. As a result, choosing between giving up JSC or VOC enhancement is a trade-off. The development of HTL and the fabrication of flexible large-area PSCs necessitates the study of innovative material systems that can improve JSC and VOC while also providing high conductivity and tunable WF. The possibility of time and money savings when scaling up the fabrication process is demonstrated by the partially delaminated multilayer MXene sheets. This suggests that manufacturing MXene materials by delamination might be feasible on a larger scale. Delaminated layers may also have improved properties that are useful in certain applications. Delamination can be used to improve the mechanical properties of composites or increase their catalytic surface area, for instance. Further research is necessary to fully comprehend how delamination affects the properties and potential applications of MXene materials. The precise effects of delamination on conductivity, interfacial properties, and other pertinent characteristics need to be investigated further. Researchers will be able to improve the fabrication process and modify MXene materials for particular applications thanks to this deeper understanding. It should be noted that because of the potent van der Waals forces between layers, MXenes have a propensity to aggregate and stack. This behavior presents a problem for the use of MXenes in devices because it can result in penetration into nearby layers and current leakage. For MXenes to be successfully incorporated into upcoming electronic and optoelectronic devices, this issue must be solved. In conclusion, MXene materials have the potential for simultaneous improvement in conductivity and interfacial properties. Delamination has the potential to help with this, but more study is necessary to fully understand its effects and improve the fabrication procedure. For the practical use of MXenes in various devices, it is also crucial to address the aggregation and stacking issues.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Industrialization of MXene
ce:section.ce:para: In fact, one of the most important aspects of research to enable the industrialization of MXene materials is preventing water and oxygen infiltration and minimizing changes in MXene performance. The following are some approaches that have been looked at to deal with this issue: one method is to alter the MXene materials' surface to form a barrier that protects against oxygen and water. The deposition of thin films or coatings that serve as barriers, such as polymers or other protective materials, may be necessary in this situation. These coatings can stop MXene from coming into direct contact with the environment, which lessens degradation. Another approach is to enclose MXene components in a shielding matrix or shell. A conformal coating can be formed around the MXene surface using methods like atomic layer deposition or by embedding MXene particles within a polymer matrix. MXene is protected from oxygen and water by a physical barrier that is aided by encapsulation. The surface area of MXene materials can be increased through nanostructuring, which shortens the distance that oxygen and water must travel to diffuse. This strategy can minimize the negative effects of water and oxygen on performance by limiting their access to MXene's interior. It is possible to lessen the degradation brought on by oxygen and water by storing or using MXene materials in a controlled setting with low humidity. It is possible to maintain the functionality of MXene materials by limiting exposure to these factors. MXene structures can have their surface properties changed by adding dopant elements or functional groups, which makes them less vulnerable to oxidative and hydroscopic degradation. The performance and stability of MXene materials under environmental exposure can be improved by doping. The use of suitable encapsulation materials, such as moisture-resistant polymers or barrier films, can be used to create an effective barrier against environmental factors, preventing water and oxygen infiltration during storage or operation. It is crucial to keep in mind that these tactics are still being researched, and their success will depend on the particular MXene material and application. To maximize the protection of MXene materials from water and oxygen degradation and support their successful industrialization, more research and development is required in this area.
